notes: Doctrine of the human person: free will, the soul, moral responsibility, and the origin and destiny of rational creatures.
Transmission of faith through apostolic succession, rule of faith, and received custom.
Practices of self-discipline including fasting and celibacy in early Christianity.
Baptismal practice and initiation.
Principles and methods of interpreting Scripture, including literal, allegorical, moral, and spiritual senses.
Doctrine that the same human bodies that die will be literally raised at the final judgment.
Geographic spread of Christianity beyond the Roman heartland — missions, frontier communities, and attestation of Christian presence in peripheral regions.
Structures of ministry authority including bishop, presbyter, and deacon.
Response demanded by Jesus ministry.
Bishop authority over sacraments and church order.
Eucharistic liturgy and assembly.
Nature and reality of Christ's presence in the Eucharist.
Expectation of a coming divine judgment at the end of the age.
Questions surrounding the admission of Gentile believers without circumcision.
Honoring sacred material objects: relics, bones, and burial places of martyrs and saints.
Doctrine that Christ truly became flesh and truly suffered; anti-docetist affirmations of real physical humanity.
Claims about the authority exercised by Jesus in teaching and action.
Public proclamation themes in the ministry narratives.
Identification of Christ with the pre-existent divine Logos/Word through whom all things were made and who became incarnate.
Person and role of Mary in Christian theology, including virgin birth and perpetual virginity.
The meaning and value of martyrdom as witness to Christ and conformity to his passion.
Claims identifying Jesus as the Christ or Messiah.
Reconciliation and restoration after post-baptismal sin.
Claims concerning the proclamation that Jesus has been raised.
Theology of the Trinity — the nature of God as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
